catalog.title: Opening Learning
catalog.subtitle: Learn Chess openings interactively: study the theory, drill variations and track your progress.

family.defaultDescription: An opening starting with the moves {{moves}}. Our database contains {{count}} variations of it that you can study and practise.

lesson.hintPiece: Hint: the piece you should move is highlighted.
lesson.yourMove: Play the book move on the board.
lesson.completed: Well done! You reached the end of the theory of this variation.

descriptions.Sicilian Defense: The most popular reply to 1.e4. Black fights for the centre asymmetrically with 1...c5, leading to rich, double-edged positions.
descriptions.Ruy Lopez: One of the oldest and most respected openings. White develops quickly and puts long-term pressure on Black's e5-pawn via the knight on c6.
descriptions.French Defense: A solid, strategic defence. Black builds the pawn chain e6-d5 and counterattacks the white centre, accepting a temporarily passive light-squared bishop.
descriptions.Queen's Gambit Declined: A classical, extremely solid answer to the Queen's Gambit. Black keeps the centre with e6 and d5 and develops harmoniously.
descriptions.Italian Game: A natural developing scheme with 3.Bc4 aiming at f7. Leads to both quiet manoeuvring games and sharp gambit play.
descriptions.English Opening: A flexible flank opening with 1.c4. White controls d5 and often transposes into many other systems.
descriptions.King's Gambit Accepted: A romantic gambit: White sacrifices the f-pawn for rapid development and attacking chances against the black king.
descriptions.King's Indian Defense: A hypermodern counterattacking system. Black allows a big white centre, then strikes back with e5 or c5 and a kingside attack.
descriptions.Caro-Kann Defense: A rock-solid reply to 1.e4. Black supports d5 with 1...c6, gets a healthy pawn structure and few weaknesses.
descriptions.Nimzo-Indian Defense: One of the best openings against 1.d4: with Bb4 Black pins the knight, fights for e4 and often damages White's pawn structure.
descriptions.Queen's Pawn Game: A broad family of systems after 1.d4 d5 without an early c4, including many quiet, positional setups.
descriptions.Dutch Defense: An ambitious defence to 1.d4. With 1...f5 Black immediately fights for e4 and aims for kingside activity.
descriptions.Semi-Slav Defense: A hybrid of the Slav and the QGD. Extremely rich in theory, with famous razor-sharp lines like the Botvinnik Variation.
descriptions.Benoni Defense: A dynamic counterattacking defence with ...c5 against 1.d4, giving Black active piece play in return for a spatial disadvantage.
descriptions.Grünfeld Defense: A hypermodern defence: Black lets White build a broad pawn centre and then attacks it with pieces and the g7-bishop.
descriptions.Queen's Gambit Accepted: Black takes the c4-pawn and gives up the centre temporarily in exchange for free development and counterplay against d4.
descriptions.Alekhine Defense: A provocative defence: the knight tempts White's pawns forward so Black can attack the overextended centre later.
descriptions.King's Gambit Declined: Black refuses the gambit pawn and calmly develops, keeping a solid position against White's aggressive f4 push.
descriptions.Queen's Indian Defense: A flexible, solid system with ...b6: Black controls the e4-square with the bishop from b7.
descriptions.Scotch Game: White opens the centre immediately with 4.d4, obtaining free piece play and clear-cut plans.
descriptions.Slav Defense: A very solid defence to the Queen's Gambit: Black defends d5 with ...c6 and keeps the light-squared bishop active.
descriptions.Petrov's Defense: A symmetrical, solid defence to 1.e4: instead of defending e5, Black counterattacks e4 at once.
descriptions.Four Knights Game: A classical, safe opening in which both sides develop their knights before deciding on a plan.
descriptions.Zukertort Opening: A flexible start with 1.Nf3 which avoids early commitments and can transpose into many different openings.
descriptions.Vienna Game: A flexible relative of the King's Gambit: White develops the queenside knight first, keeping the f4 push in reserve.
descriptions.Scandinavian Defense: Black challenges the centre immediately with 1...d5, simplifying the structure at the cost of early queen moves.
descriptions.Pirc Defense: A hypermodern defence: Black develops modestly with g6 and Bg7, inviting White to overextend in the centre.
descriptions.London System: A popular, easy-to-learn system for White: Bf4, e3 and a solid pawn triangle, playable against almost anything.
